caveats: a: The occupation evidence covers the broader dairy-manufacturing group.
a: Counts are not wage-weighted task measures and require judgmental mapping.
a: Physical automation and robotics are excluded unless AI directly avoids labor or hiring.
rho: Regulatory guidance shows requirements and feasible computerized functions, not observed AI conversion rates.
rho: Branded operators with rich sell-through data may implement faster than private-label plants dependent on customer forecasts.
e: The frozen LMM count is accepted without re-estimation.
e: The NAICS definition describes production activities but not recurring-customer quality, independence, or enterprise transferability.
s5: The owner-age statistic is national, cross-industry, and based on 2018 data.
s5: It measures responding owners rather than firms and does not observe transactions.
q: No public source directly measures retention of AI-derived benefits in this industry.
q: Branded premium products can retain much more than private-label or commodity-like frozen novelties.
q: Shrinkflation and promotion changes can obscure true pass-through.
d5: Production reflects inventories, imports, exports, and capacity as well as end demand.
d5: USDA's cited series emphasizes frozen dairy and may not fully represent nondairy products included in NAICS 311520.
d5: Aggregate decline can coexist with growth in premium, novelty, private-label, or nondairy niches.
o: This estimates accountable-operator need, not employment intensity.
o: Operator responsibility may consolidate into larger manufacturers even though software cannot eliminate it.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat unit- or volume-priced product sales through retail, foodservice, distributor, branded, club, and private-label channels, often with seasonal demand, promotions, slotting, and cold-chain requirements.
lens.excluded_activities: Frozen bakery products, dairy farms, scoop shops or restaurants without qualifying manufacturing, captive internal plants without external customers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market operators that repeatedly manufacture ice cream, frozen yogurt, frozen ices, sherbet, frozen tofu, and other frozen desserts for external retail, foodservice, distributor, private-label, or branded customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for broad NAICS 311500 dairy manufacturing, not wage-weighted unautomated task exposure in frozen-dessert manufacturing. The estimate maps forecasting, seasonal scheduling, promotion and SKU planning, quality-document review, maintenance support, procurement, customer service, and administration to higher AI exposure while assigning little exposure to hands-on batching, freezing, filling, sanitation, warehousing, maintenance, and driving. The proxy may overstate exposure where packaged plants already automate planning and controls, or understate it where seasonal SKU proliferation creates unusually heavy coordination and merchandising work.
caveats: The occupation evidence covers the broader dairy-manufacturing group.
Counts are not wage-weighted task measures and require judgmental mapping.
Physical automation and robotics are excluded unless AI directly avoids labor or hiring.
a.change_evidence: A 311520 payroll-by-SOC extract and wage-weighted task study covering demand planning, SKU setup, trade promotion, procurement, quality, maintenance, customer service, and plant administration, plus an inventory of existing planning, ERP, MES, warehouse, and process automation.
a.rationale: The broad dairy occupation mix is physical: BLS reports 25,520 food batchmakers, 23,690 packaging/filling operators, 8,170 material movers, and 7,840 production supervisors. Frozen dessert operations add forecast, assortment, promotion, and cold-chain complexity suited to AI, but the core manufacturing and sanitation work remains embodied.

d5.bridge: USDA ERS measures U.S. production volume for ice cream and other frozen dairy products, not constant-quality demand for the exact current basket supplied by eligible LMM firms and not all nondairy frozen desserts in 311520. It reports a 10% decline from a 2003 peak of 1,542 million gallons to 1,386 million gallons in 2024 and renewed decline after 2022; regular ice cream remained 60-65% of production while category mix shifted. The five-year range translates that mature, declining volume signal into a modest base decline but allows specialty, premium, nondairy, and private-label share gains. It may overstate lens decline if LMM firms disproportionately serve growing niches and understate it if volume consolidates into national scale processors.
caveats: Production reflects inventories, imports, exports, and capacity as well as end demand.
USDA's cited series emphasizes frozen dairy and may not fully represent nondairy products included in NAICS 311520.
Aggregate decline can coexist with growth in premium, novelty, private-label, or nondairy niches.
d5.change_evidence: USDA ERS and NASS five-year production and per-capita series by format, Circana or NielsenIQ constant-volume retail scanner data, Technomic foodservice volumes, private-label share, customer forecasts, nondairy category shipments, and eligible-firm sell-through cohorts.
d5.rationale: The long-run and post-2022 production trend argues for a cautious real-volume base case. Demand remains culturally durable and mix can migrate toward regular, premium, low-sugar, portion-controlled, or nondairy products, but mature consumption, health preferences, price sensitivity, and large-brand concentration constrain growth.

caveats: The frozen LMM count is accepted without re-estimation.
The NAICS definition describes production activities but not recurring-customer quality, independence, or enterprise transferability.
e.change_evidence: A complete 311520 LMM firm list matched to ownership, manufacturing versus retail revenue, private-label and branded mix, customer concentration, standalone management, cold-chain assets, normalized EBITDA, brand and license transferability, and captive status.
e.rationale: The industry is an external product-supply activity with branded and private-label recurrence, and its varied formats permit multiple independent LMM operators. Eligibility is reduced by captive strategic plants, enterprises that are primarily retail foodservice, customer concentration, nontransferable brand licenses, subscale seasonal operations, and facilities whose value depends on a founder or single retailer.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311520 ($1,444,332,744) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311520 ($11,346,498,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $484,893 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311520); class <5: 186 firms, avg revenue/firm $558,645 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $85,193 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 83 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,343,470 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $357,379 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 68 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,065,103 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $467,428 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 69 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,265,406 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,260,474 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $48,630,524 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,416,155 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $369,924,040 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $56,413,416 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 90

caveats: This estimates accountable-operator need, not employment intensity.
Operator responsibility may consolidate into larger manufacturers even though software cannot eliminate it.
o.change_evidence: Retailer and foodservice make-versus-buy plans, announced customer or strategic manufacturing capacity, co-manufacturing share trends, import penetration, direct-to-consumer self-production, and regulatory changes affecting autonomous release or remote plant oversight.
o.rationale: Frozen desserts are physical, temperature-controlled food products requiring an accountable manufacturer to formulate, freeze, package, test, release, store, and trace output. Federal standards specify physical composition for ice cream, and software cannot supply the basket by itself. Only customer self-manufacture, vertical integration, imports, or substitution into nonmanufactured alternatives reduces the operator-required share.

caveats: No public source directly measures retention of AI-derived benefits in this industry.
Branded premium products can retain much more than private-label or commodity-like frozen novelties.
Shrinkflation and promotion changes can obscure true pass-through.
q.change_evidence: Retailer, distributor, foodservice, and co-manufacturing contracts; open-book and productivity-sharing clauses; promotion and rebate history; private-label bid files; customer concentration; branded versus contract gross margins; and realized pricing after prior productivity improvements.
q.rationale: Unit-priced branded and differentiated products can retain workflow savings through brand equity, formulation, innovation, service levels, and consumer willingness to pay. Retailer concentration, private-label bids, commodity and freight negotiations, trade-promotion demands, slotting, renewal repricing, and competing adoption pass some benefit to customers over five years.

caveats: Regulatory guidance shows requirements and feasible computerized functions, not observed AI conversion rates.
Branded operators with rich sell-through data may implement faster than private-label plants dependent on customer forecasts.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level validated pilots, forecast-accuracy and waste baselines, schedule-change and overtime logs, ERP/MES/WMS integration maps, allergen and release controls, cybersecurity reviews, customer audit findings, and interviews with QA and operations leaders.
rho.rationale: Demand forecasting, seasonal scheduling, promotion analysis, document review, customer service, maintenance support, and quality-data triage are deployable within five years. Legacy systems, noisy promotional data, food-safety validation, label and allergen risk, cybersecurity, integration with plant controls, and human review limit implementation. Computerized food-processing controls are accepted only when regulatory intent and safety-critical performance are maintained.

s5.bridge: The Census Annual Business Survey infographic reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses were age 55 or older in 2018; it does not measure frozen-dessert control transfers. The estimate converts a minority of age-related succession exposure into qualifying five-year transfers after allowing for owners who continue, family succession, minority recapitalizations, multi-owner firms, and nontransferable businesses. It may overstate events among institutional or strategic owners and understate strategic platform and brand acquisitions in a fragmented specialty-food niche.
caveats: The owner-age statistic is national, cross-industry, and based on 2018 data.
It measures responding owners rather than firms and does not observe transactions.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, and food-industry M&A histories tagged to ice cream and frozen desserts; direct owner-age and succession mapping; and interviews with food-focused lenders, brokers, and strategic buyers.
s5.rationale: Succession pressure and strategic interest in brands, private label, and cold-chain capacity create plausible transfer supply, but only a minority of eligible operators should complete a qualifying control sale within five years.

principal_driver: Seasonal planning complexity, differentiated brands, and repeat retail and foodservice demand create deployable information-work opportunities.
principal_weakness: Mature declining volume and physical cold-chain production limit durable growth and software-only labor substitution.

run_meta.title: Ice Cream and Frozen Dessert Manufacturing

sources.supports: Defines 311520 as manufacturing ice cream, frozen yogurt, frozen ices, sherbet, frozen tofu, and other frozen desserts, while excluding frozen bakery products.
sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: 311520 Ice Cream and Frozen Dessert Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports the largest dairy-manufacturing occupations, including 25,520 food batchmakers, 23,690 packaging/filling operators, 8,170 material movers, and 7,840 production supervisors.
sources.title: BLS OEWS Data Tables for Industry Charts, May 2024

sources.supports: Describes computerized food-processing records and controls and review expectations for regulatory equivalence and safety-critical functions.
sources.title: FDA: Computerized Systems in the Food Processing Industry

sources.supports: Describes regulated dairy responsibilities for processing times and temperatures, equipment control and testing, worker safety, laboratory testing, and oversight.
sources.title: FDA: Keeping Your Milk Safe From the Grass to the Glass

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 ABS covering data year 2018.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: Reports a 10% decline from the 2003 peak of 1,542 million gallons to 1,386 million gallons in 2024, renewed decline after 2022, and regular ice cream at about 60-65% of output.
sources.title: USDA ERS: Ice cream and frozen dairy production dipped from 2000 to 2024

sources.supports: Reports 1.3 billion gallons of U.S. ice cream production in 2023 and a decline in aggregate frozen-dairy output from 1.5 billion gallons annually in 1999-2003 to 1.4 billion in 2019-2023.
sources.title: USDA ERS: Low-fat and nonfat ice cream production is heating up the market

sources.supports: Specifies physical composition and weight requirements for ice cream, including minimum total solids, milk solids, and milkfat.
sources.title: USDA AMS Ice Cream Standard

title: Ice Cream and Frozen Dessert Manufacturing
